title: Python Variables
summary: Understand variables, data types, and memory references in Python.
intuition: Variables are containers for values. In ML workflows, you constantly tune variables like learning_rate, epochs, and batch_size.

title: NumPy Arrays
summary: Work with efficient arrays for numerical ML computation.
code:
import numpy as np
features = np.array([[1.2, 3.1], [0.7, 2.8]])
weights = np.array([0.4, 0.9])
predictions = features @ weights
print(predictions)
intuition: Arrays let you process many values at once. This vectorized style is the backbone of fast ML and deep learning.

title: Vectors
summary: Build geometric intuition for vectors in feature spaces.
code:
import numpy as np
v = np.array([3, 4])
length = np.linalg.norm(v)
print(length)
intuition: Each ML example can be seen as a vector of features. Geometry helps you understand distances and similarities.

title: Dot Product
summary: Measure alignment and combine weighted features.
code:
import numpy as np
x = np.array([2, 1, 3])
w = np.array([0.5, -1.2, 0.8])
score = np.dot(x, w)
print(score)
intuition: A dot product tells you how aligned two vectors are. Linear models use it to turn features into predictions.

title: Gradient Descent
summary: Learn iterative optimization for minimizing model error.
code:
w = 10
learning_rate = 0.1
for step in range(5):
    grad = 2 * w
    w = w - learning_rate * grad
    print(step, w)
intuition: Gradient descent follows the slope downhill. Repeating small updates improves model parameters over time.

title: Linear Regression
summary: Fit a line to model relationships between variables.
code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
X = [[1], [2], [3], [4]]
y = [2, 4, 6, 8]
model = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)
print(model.coef_, model.intercept_)
intuition: Linear regression finds the best-fitting line by minimizing squared error. It is the foundation for more advanced ML models.
